ams_rationale: 11 (number theory: smooth numbers / distribution of integers free of large prime factors, 11N25)
divergence_notes: INTERPRETATION (statement vs problem text): the text as written is trivially true (the site commentary itself says so: take {1,...,k} and n > k^{1/eps}). The drafted statement formalizes the commentary's SECOND non-trivial variant -- the k consecutive integers must lie in [n/2, n] -- which is what the upstream 'proved (Lean)' status refers to (SkyYang via the Balog-Wooley construction) and what BOTH hosted theorems prove. The literal text has no [n/2,n] constraint; the window is a strengthening taken from the site commentary and is documented in the docstring.

HOSTED-vs-HOSTED disagreement on the smoothness bound: jayyhk bounds every prime factor by N^eps (the problem text's n^eps-smooth); plby bounds prime factors of each element m = a - j by m^eps ((a-j)^eps), i.e. the commentary's FIRST variant per-element bound, a strengthening since m <= N. Drafted statement follows the text (and jayyhk): largest prime factor <= (n : R)^eps.

Smoothness expressed as `forall p in (a+1+j).primeFactors, (p : R) <= (n : R)^eps` instead of the hosted custom `Nat.largestPrimeFactor` (not a Mathlib definition; jayyhk defines it ad hoc, sup of primeFactors). Equivalent: sup over primeFactors <= x iff every prime factor <= x, vacuous for m <= 1, and all elements here are >= 1.

Run parametrized ascending as {a+1, ..., a+k} with `n / 2 <= a + 1 && a + k <= n`, avoiding N truncated subtraction; both hosted use the descending form {a-(k-1), ..., a} with an extra guard `k <= a`. The substitution a_hosted = a_draft + k is a bijection between witnesses; the `k <= a` guard has no analogue needed in the ascending form.
'for all sufficiently large n' rendered as `forall-eventually n in atTop` (idiomatic Mathlib); both hosted use `exists N0, forall N, N0 <= N -> ...` (equivalent by definition of atTop eventually).
Window lower endpoint uses natural floor division n / 2, exactly as in both hosted theorems; the commentary writes '[n/2,n] (say)', so the endpoint convention is explicitly immaterial.

Statement form: FC house style `answer(True) <-> ...` with eps and k universally quantified inside the iff (the problem is a solved yes/no question); both hosted state the bare proposition with eps, k as theorem parameters (same content, different packaging).
